title: Recovering Labels from Local Updates in Federated Learning
abstract: Gradient inversion (GI) attacks present a threat to the privacy of clients in federated learning (FL) by aiming to enable reconstruction of the clients’ data from communicated model updates. A number of such techniques attempts to accelerate data recovery by first reconstructing labels of the samples used in local training. However, existing label extraction methods make strong assumptions that typically do not hold in realistic FL settings. In this paper we present a novel label recovery scheme, Recovering Labels from Local Updates (RLU), which provides near-perfect accuracy when attacking untrained (most vulnerable) models. More significantly, RLU achieves high performance even in realistic real-world settings where the clients in an FL system run multiple local epochs, train on heterogeneous data, and deploy various optimizers to minimize different objective functions. Specifically, RLU estimates labels by solving a least-square problem that emerges from the analysis of the correlation between labels of the data points used in a training round and the resulting update of the output layer. The experimental results on several datasets, architectures, and data heterogeneity scenarios demonstrate that the proposed method consistently outperforms existing baselines, and helps improve quality of the reconstructed images in GI attacks in terms of both PSNR and LPIPS.
impact: Our paper investigates a novel method for label recovery attacks on Federated Learning (FL), with the goal of revealing security vulnerabilities in FL and no intentions to exploit these vulnerabilities to harm the community. As demonstrated in the paper, a straightforward way to limit the vulnerability to attacks by RLU is achieved by increasing the number of local epochs and injecting differentially private (DP) noise to the gradients. Nevertheless, there are many other defense mechanisms such as gradient sparsification and secure aggregation that can be utilized to defend against label recovery attacks. We are committed to exploring the defense to attacks on FL in our future work.

query_input.scenario: Label leakage via linear layer analysis of local updates
query_input.issue: Recovery of ground-truth labels and augmented soft labels from single-input gradients of the output layer
hazard: Gradient inversion attacks enable exact reconstruction of private training data from shared model updates in federated learning.
exposure: Clients sharing gradient updates containing label information in federated training

hipporag_result.vulnerability: Output layer gradients encode a linear correlation with labels that persists across realistic training settings, enabling inversion via least squares.
hipporag_result.impact: Private client training data is reconstructed with high fidelity, compromising data confidentiality.
hipporag_result.dose_response: Gradient inversion attacks, through clients sharing label-informative gradient updates in federated training, trigger output layer gradients' linear label correlation enabling least-squares inversion, leading to high-fidelity reconstruction of private training data, compromising confidentiality.

dose_response: Label leakage from gradient updates enables adversaries to infer sensitive attributes like medical conditions, violating confidentiality laws and enabling insider trading, which reduces trust and participation in federated systems.
vulnerability: Clients with small, imbalanced datasets produce gradients that directly encode label distributions.
Organizations in regulated sectors (healthcare, finance) face compounded harm when labels reveal protected attributes.
impact: Inference of medical diagnoses from label leakage violates patient confidentiality laws.
Extracted financial labels enable insider trading or credit discrimination.
Loss of user trust reduces participation in federated systems, undermining collaborative model quality.
